input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle used for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) research. It is a teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control hardware, consisting of lower and upper chassis levels and embedded autonomy elements (e.g., perception, decision and actuation modules). The platform operates in a controlled test environment and provides vehicle state, projected path and surrounding object tracks, making it suitable for digital‑twin‑based monitoring, off‑loading and remote management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path and detected object tracks, allows users to view, save and generate reports of the data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, distance‑based speed computation) runs on the DT in the cloud and returns the desired speed to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set a target speed and issue an emergency‑brake command through the UI, with low‑latency bi‑directional communication to the vehicle. The services target two user classes: operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting and data analysis).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT is built following the ISO 23247‑2 entity‑based reference architecture, comprising a Data Collection and Device Control Entity, a Digital Twin Entity and a User Entity made up of functional entities (data acquisition, transformation, actuation and UI). Implementation uses the open‑source Automotive‑Digital‑Twin prototype hosted at McSCert (GitHub repository). Cloud resources host the ACC computation for off‑loading, while a UI (likely web‑based) provides visualization and control. The architecture allows OPC Unified Architecture or similar middleware for data exchange, and relies on low‑latency networking to satisfy safety‑critical requirements.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The twin requires clock synchronization between physical and digital components within 100 ms, as dictated by hardware limitations and safety‑critical vehicular standards. All ROS2 nodes exchange messages using DDS, which is configured to meet this sub‑100 ms bound, supporting the low end‑to‑end latency needed for data monitoring, computation offloading and remote management services.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS consists of a single instance of three coordinated entities – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity – each deployed on separate hardware modules and implemented as distinct ROS2 packages containing one or more nodes. The hierarchy is: Physical Twin Entity hosts sensors, actuators and compute resources; Digital Twin Entity hosts data management and control logic; User Entity provides the 3‑D visualization and control‑panel UI. No multiple twin instances or decentralized replication are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture using an entity‑based constellation. Functional Entities are mapped to ROS2 packages; inter‑entity communication is handled by ROS2 DDS middleware, enabling real‑time data flow and control commands. Resource allocation is performed per entity on dedicated hardware, and the overall orchestration is achieved by the ROS2 launch system that starts the three packages together, forming a coherent digital twin system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DTS uses a behavioral model of the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) algorithm that determines the leading vehicle and computes a desired speed based on inter‑vehicle distance. This model runs in the Digital Twin Entity and receives pre‑processed sensor data (vehicle state, object tracks) via the Data Translation Functional Entity, which serialises data, aligns message types and performs unit conversions for every data stream. Geometric data is derived from LiDAR point‑cloud clustering and tracking, providing object positions and trajectories used by the ACC model for path projection. All data streams are managed as ROS2 topics within a ROS2 network; persistent data such as logs and generated reports are stored by the Data Storage component. Model relationships: LiDAR point‑cloud → Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking FE → Data Pre‑Processing FE → Data Translation FE → ACC Controlling FE → actuation command. The models are instantiated as ROS2 nodes packaged per functional entity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the physical twin is performed by a motor (for vehicle propulsion) and a steering servo. The Digital Twin Entity sends the desired speed computed by the ACC model back to the vehicle where the Motor Actuation FE applies the speed command, and the Steering Servo Actuation FE adjusts steering angle. Remote control commands (enable/disable ACC, set speed, emergency brake) are issued from the User Interface FE through the Control Panel and transmitted over a Wi‑Fi UDP link to the Physical Twin Entity. The actuation range includes the full speed envelope of the 1/10‑scale vehicle and the full steering range of the servo; safety constraints are enforced by requiring 100 ms clock‑synchronisation accuracy for command delivery, as stated in the architecture requirements.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing stack consists of a LiDAR sensor that provides raw point‑cloud data; the point‑cloud is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking functional entity to generate object tracks. Vehicle state information (e.g., speed, position) is also collected, likely via on‑board CAN, and fed into the Vehicle State functional entity. Sensor data is published as ROS2 topics and routed through the Data Pre‑Processing FE. All sensors are physically mounted on the 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle and communicate with the Physical Twin Entity, which hosts the resource‑specific functionality. Data is transmitted over the ROS2 network to the Digital Twin Entity and over Wi‑Fi UDP between entities.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is driven by the need for low end‑to‑end latency (≤100 ms) and safety‑critical operation. The ACC behavioural model is validated against real vehicle state and object tracking data obtained from the LiDAR clustering component. Uncertainty sources include sensor noise from LiDAR and vehicle state measurements; the Data Translation FE reduces systematic errors by performing unit conversion and message alignment. Validation is performed by visual inspection of real‑time 3D visualisation and by generating reports that compare predicted paths with actual vehicle trajectories. ROS2 communication integrity and UDP packet loss are mitigated by the required 100 ms clock synchronization and by monitoring data timestamps. Quality assurance includes a publicly available demonstration video and compliance with ISO 23247 functional entities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the Physical Twin (LiDAR point‑cloud, motor status, steering servo position, vehicle state) is first captured by the Data Pre‑processing FE (e.g., the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component) where raw point clouds are clustered and tracked. The processed data is then handed to the Data Translation FE, which serialises the streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions as required by ISO 23247. Within the Digital Twin Entity the components communicate over a ROS 2 network; inter‑entity communication (Physical Twin ↔ Digital Twin ↔ User Entity) uses a Wi‑Fi link with UDP at the transport layer. The flow carries vehicle‑state information, object tracks and the projected driving path to the 3D Visualization FE for real‑time display. No explicit transmission frequency, event triggers or additional data‑quality checks are documented.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Commands generated by the Digital Twin (desired speed, ACC enable/disable, emergency‑braking command) are produced by the Control Panel FE in the User Entity, passed through the Data Translation FE for serialisation and unit conversion, and transmitted back to the Physical Twin over the same Wi‑Fi UDP channel. The Actuation FE on the Physical Twin receives these control messages and forwards the desired speed to the vehicle’s actuators (motor, steering servo). The documents do not detail specific command message formats, validation, authentication, conflict‑resolution policies, or explicit execution acknowledgements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a hybrid communication stack: intra‑entity software components are coupled via a ROS 2 network, while communication between the three ISO 23247 entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) is carried out over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP. The design requires low end‑to‑end latency (the system is described as needing “low end‑to‑end latency communication”) and a clock‑synchronisation accuracy of 100 ms for safety‑critical operation. No bandwidth figures, redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols (e.g., encryption, authentication) are specified in the provided text.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The ACC functionality is off‑loaded from the on‑board ECU to an external compute platform described only as “the cloud”. Each functional element is packaged as a ROS 2 package (one or more nodes) and deployed on separate hardware components corresponding to the three entities. The documents do not give concrete details on storage capacity, compute specifications, scalability approaches, deployment models (e.g., containerisation, edge vs. cloud), or availability/reliability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The automotive digital twin system adheres to the ISO 23247 reference architecture, structuring functionality into the Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity and their associated Functional Entities. Safety aspects are aligned with ISO 26262, which the authors recommend promoting to a first‑class citizen in automotive DT standards. Interoperability and data exchange are implemented using OPC Unified Architecture as defined in IEC 62541 for the Data Translation functional entity. The software components are realized as ROS 2 packages, complying with the ROS 2 middleware specifications, and the overall design acknowledges the AUTOSAR automotive software architecture for hardware‑agnostic component deployment.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]

Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact

(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.

_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_
block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations

1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)


(UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation of the Data
Translation FE, or implement a custom communication layer.
Although the standard was developed for the manufacturing
domain, its RA is an appealing feature for DT developers in
other domains. Shtofenmakher and Shao [16] adapt the RA
of ISO 23247 to space debris detection. In their approach,
the monolithic User Interface FE is decomposed into four
distinct FEs, each providing a specific viewpoint for space
data systems, public users, and satellite owners and operators,
who need to interact with the DT in very different ways. Since
not all space objects are controllable, the OME is decomposed
into controllable and uncontrollable Resident Space Objects.
Both types of OMEs are able to provide data through the
Data Collection and Control Entity. Cederbladh et al. [17]
investigate the fitness of the RA for DTs of heterogeneous
battery systems in electric vehicles. In their proposal, the DT
Entity is split into two components to execute on separate
hardware. A lightweight real-time component deals with timesensitive computation and an offline component deals with
computation-intensive tasks. Liu and David [15] investigate the
fitness of the RA for AI simulation, the technique of training
AI agents through simulated data. The proliferation of DTs has
had a positive impact on simulator technology too: today, some
of the most advanced simulators are found in DTs, making an
appealing case for implementing AI simulation by DTs. In
addition, DTs can collect data from the physical twin through
purposeful experimentation [18]. The _Collection Identification_
_FE_ is an apt spot to implement such features, while AI agents
can integrate with the DT through the User Entity.
These works underscore the utility of the ISO 23247 RA
outside manufacturing. Of course, part of this utility is due
to the lack of alternatives. Nonetheless, by properly mapping
domain-specific requirements, the RA can serve as a useful
starting point in the development of DTs outside of manufacturing. Our work is the first attempt at investigating the fitness
of the ISO 23247 RA for automotive systems.


_B. Automotive digital twins_

Functional safety,” Tech. Rep. ISO 26262-1:2018, Dec. 2018.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
